summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that an unauthenticated attacker can make the crawl4ai server fetch and return content from internal services. A mock internal HTTP server serving a proof string was started inside the container on localhost:8888, and the POST /md endpoint successfully fetched and returned its content.
expected_security_impact: Running the reproduction should cause the crawl4ai server to fetch content from the mock internal service (localhost:8888) and return it in the /md response, demonstrating Server-Side Request Forgery.
actual_observed_result: The /md endpoint returned the content of the mock internal service, including the proof string EVOHUNT_SSRF_PROOF_7f3a9c2b, confirming that the server can be made to access internal services without authentication.
verification_method: The verify.sh script checks that the /md response body contains the unique proof string 'EVOHUNT_SSRF_PROOF_7f3a9c2b' served by the mock internal service. This string is only accessible from within the container's localhost network, confirming the SSRF. A generic crawl failure or startup error would not contain this string.
evidence_produced_by_run: The /md endpoint response contained the proof string EVOHUNT_SSRF_PROOF_7f3a9c2b from the internal mock service.
The internal service was bound to 127.0.0.1:8888, inaccessible from outside the container.
No authentication token was required to exploit the /md endpoint.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable tag, and uses it to build the base crawl4ai Docker image. A second build step layers the exploit scripts on top.
